skill.name: nextjs-app-router-patterns
skill.description: Guides Next.js 14+ App Router work with Server Components, streaming, route handlers, caching, metadata, and migration patterns.
skill.summary: Apply proven Next.js App Router patterns for modern full-stack React applications.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, network, and environment access patterns. Review found the command, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance alerts are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, file names, comments, and normal Next.js examples. The skill is safe to publish with low risk because it contains documentation only, but some copyable examples need validation and authorization hardening.

low_findings.title: Copyable Route Handler Examples Lack Input Validation
low_findings.description: The route handler example parses request JSON and passes the body directly into a database create operation. This is documentation, not active code, but users could copy it without schema validation, authorization checks, or field allowlisting.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example directly uses request body data in a database create call. Risk is limited because the skill is Markdown guidance, not executable package code.

low_findings.title: Form Handling Example Shows Minimal Validation
low_findings.description: The Server Action checkout example only checks that address and payment fields exist before processing an order. This may understate validation, authorization, and payment-handling requirements in production code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example has clear minimal validation around sensitive checkout data. It is a moderate documentation concern rather than evidence of malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The scanner interpreted triple backticks in Markdown examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These locations are documentation delimiters and resource references, not executable code paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is inside Markdown documentation or inline code formatting. No script file or runtime command execution primitive is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Calls Are Illustrative Next.js Fetch Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The fetch calls and hardcoded links are ordinary examples for Next.js data fetching and references to public documentation. No credential exfiltration or hidden outbound call is present.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network examples use api.example.com, a configurable API_URL, generic url variables, and official resource links. I found no evidence of secret collection or suspicious destination domains.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Variable Access Is Configuration Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The process.env.API_URL reference demonstrates server-side API configuration. It does not read credential files or transmit secrets in the skill itself.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The only environment access is a sample base URL in a fenced TypeScript example. No environment file access or secret exfiltration logic exists in the skill files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Textual False Positives
dangerous_patterns.description: The scanner flagged normal words and code-example identifiers as weak cryptography or reconnaissance. Review found no cryptographic operations, system probes, or host inspection commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found no md5, sha1, crypto APIs, uname, whoami, or similar reconnaissance commands. The cited lines are Next.js prose or TypeScript metadata examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The code-execution, network, and credential-access combination is not confirmed. The underlying signals are Markdown formatting, illustrative fetch calls, and a configuration variable inside documentation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The heuristic depends on several static signals that are not semantically dangerous here. I found no malicious intent, prompt injection, executable payload, or data exfiltration path.

content.value_statement: Next.js App Router projects can become hard to structure as Server Components, streaming, and caching interact. This skill provides focused patterns and examples for building maintainable full-stack React routes.
actual_capabilities: Explains App Router file conventions such as layout, page, loading, error, and route files.
Shows Server Component data fetching with Suspense and streaming boundaries.
Provides examples for Server Actions, route handlers, parallel routes, and intercepting routes.
Covers Next.js cache settings, tag revalidation, path revalidation, and static parameters.
Guides metadata generation for product-style dynamic routes.
limitations: It is guidance content and does not run migrations or modify a project by itself.
Examples are generic and need project-specific validation, authorization, and error handling.
It focuses on Next.js 14+ App Router, not legacy Pages Router architecture.
It does not replace official framework documentation for version-specific API changes.

use_cases.title: Plan a New App Router Project
use_cases.description: Use the skill to choose route structure, Server Component boundaries, loading states, and caching behavior before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Migrate Pages Router Features
use_cases.description: Map existing pages, API routes, and data fetching patterns to App Router files, route handlers, and Server Actions.

use_cases.title: Review Next.js Architecture
use_cases.description: Compare a codebase against documented patterns for streaming, parallel routes, metadata, and cache invalidation.

prompt_templates.title: Explain the App Router Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how I should structure a small Next.js App Router project with layout, page, loading, error, and route files.

prompt_templates.title: Design Server Component Data Fetching
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Server Component data fetching pattern for a product listing page with filters, pagination, Suspense, and cache tags.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Pages Router Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me migrate an existing Pages Router feature to App Router. Include route structure, data loading, mutations, and validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an App Router Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Next.js App Router architecture for Server Component boundaries, streaming, route handlers, caching, metadata, and security gaps.

output_examples.input: Plan a product listing page with filters and cache invalidation.
output: A route layout using Server Components for data loading and Client Components for filters.
A Suspense boundary around the product list with a dedicated loading state.
A tag-based cache invalidation plan for product updates.

output_examples.input: Review a checkout Server Action pattern.
output: A checklist covering required fields, schema validation, authentication, authorization, and error handling.
Guidance to keep payment secrets server-side and avoid trusting client-submitted totals.
Recommended revalidation and redirect behavior after a successful order.

output_examples.input: Migrate a modal route from Pages Router.
output: An intercepting route plan for modal navigation and full-page fallback rendering.
Parallel route placement for modal content and a default fallback file.
Verification steps for direct links, refresh behavior, and browser navigation.

best_practices: Start with Server Components and add Client Components only for browser APIs, hooks, or direct interactivity.
Validate all route handler bodies, Server Action inputs, search parameters, and path parameters before use.
Define cache behavior deliberately with revalidation, tags, paths, and Suspense boundaries.
anti_patterns: Copying generic route handler examples without authentication, authorization, or schema validation.
Moving data fetching into Client Components when Server Components or route handlers fit better.
Adding nested layouts and parallel routes before the navigation model requires them.

faq.question: Does this skill support Next.js 14 and newer?
faq.answer: Yes. It focuses on Next.js 14+ App Router patterns and modern React Server Components.

faq.question: Can it migrate my project automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides planning guidance and examples, but a developer must apply changes in the codebase.

faq.question: Does it cover Server Actions?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Server Action examples for mutations, redirects, and cache revalidation.

faq.question: Does it include security guidance?
faq.answer: It includes general implementation patterns. Production code still needs validation, authorization, and secret handling review.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is it only for new projects?
faq.answer: No. It also helps with App Router migrations and architecture reviews for existing applications.
